title: Ultra-High Dynamic Strength of Additively Manufactured GRX-810 Under Coupled Conditions of High Strain Rate and Elevated Temperature
abstract: Deformation mechanisms in CrCoNi-based oxide-dispersion-strengthened multi-principal element alloys (CrCoNi-based ODS-MPEA) have been extensively studied under quasi-static and low strain rate loading over a wide temperature range, yet their behavior at high strain rates and elevated temperatures remains poorly understood. In this work, we investigate the high strain rate response of the CrCoNi-based ODS-MPEA alloy GRX-810 and its non-ODS variant. The ODS variant contains a high density of hexagonal yttria nanoparticles that serve as the strengthening oxide phase. At high strain rates and ambient temperature, GRX-810 ODS exhibits higher dynamic strength, approximately 2.79 times its quasi-static strength, than both conventional alloys and its non-ODS variant because of the additional athermal strengthening provided by the nanoscale oxide dispersion. At high strain rates and elevated temperatures, however, GRX-810 ODS undergoes thermal softening. This response is consistent with dislocation confinement associated with the small interparticle spacing of the oxide dispersion, which limits the phonon-drag contribution, together with the temperature-dependent reduction of elastic constants that lowers the athermal strengthening terms, including the oxide-related contribution. Additional weakening of the solute-pinning mechanism at elevated temperature further reduces the dynamic yield strength.
summary: This paper investigates how adding nanoscale yttria particles to a CrCoNi-based alloy affects its strength during ultra-high-speed impacts at high temperatures. While the particles provide extra strength at room temperature through the Orowan mechanism, they actually cause a larger drop in strength when heated because they prevent dislocations from moving long enough to benefit from phonon-drag effects. This research is crucial for designing materials for extreme environments like rocket engines.
main_problem: Understanding the deformation mechanisms and dynamic strength of additively manufactured CrCoNi-based oxide-dispersion-strengthened (ODS) alloys under the coupled conditions of ultra-high strain rates and elevated temperatures.
main_result: The ODS variant exhibits significantly higher dynamic strength at ambient temperature due to athermal Orowan strengthening, but undergoes pronounced thermal softening at elevated temperatures because the oxide-particle network limits dislocation glide distance, suppressing the phonon-drag contribution.
method: Laser-induced particle impact testing (LIPIT) using silica microparticles and high-speed videography, combined with a semi-empirical scaling model and a dislocation dynamics framework to decompose strength contributions.
model_or_system: The physical system is an additively manufactured CrCoNi-based ODS-MPEA (GRX-810) containing yttria nanoparticles. The modeling uses a strength summation framework including thermal, athermal, solute, and dislocation-drag components, alongside the Gillis and Kratochvil framework for dislocation acceleration.
key_observables: Particle impact and rebound velocities, Coefficient of Restitution (CoR), dynamic yield strength, and grain size evolution.
important_parameters: Strain rates of 10^6 to 10^7 s^-1, temperatures of 20 C and 155 C, interparticle spacing of ~70 nm, and impact velocities of 80-210 m/s.
main_assumptions: The oxide particles are assumed to be thermally stable without coarsening, and the impact response is modeled assuming elastic-perfectly plastic behavior of the substrate.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the LPBF fabrication and LIPIT setup; Fig 2 shows in situ high-speed images of particle impact and rebound; Fig 5d compares the required glide distance for drag development against the actual interparticle spacing.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the material and problem, describes the LIPIT experimental setup and fabrication, presents results for dynamic strength at different temperatures, develops a mechanistic model for dislocation-drag suppression, and concludes with the implications for extreme environment applications.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Overview of material fabrication and high strain rate testing. (a) Schematic of the laser powder bed fusion (LPBF) process used to produce GRX-810 in both ODS and non-ODS conditions. (b) Schematic of the laser induced particle impact testing (LIPIT) setup used to probe the dynamic response of GRX-810 under extreme high strain rate loading. Methods The high strain rate impact experiments were carried out using LIPIT (Fig. 1b), which is a platform consisting of four main components: launching laser, launch pad assembly, target stage, and an in situ optical imaging system for resolving particle impact and rebound.
